output_text:
Summary: Critique of the related-work/results section, flagging a load-bearing internal contradiction and several other issues to fix before submission.

# Critical issues

1. **Your headline claim contradicts your own related work.** You cite Liu and Zhang (2019) as reporting **91.2% AUROC at 50% coverage** on ChestX-ray14, then claim **87.4% AUROC at 50% coverage** is "the strongest published result on this benchmark to date." 87.4 < 91.2. Either:
   - The Liu and Zhang number is wrong/misremembered/fabricated — verify the citation. If you cannot find the paper, remove the claim.
   - The comparison is not apples-to-apples (different split, different pathology subset, different metric definition) — in which case you must say so explicitly and not claim SOTA without qualification.
   - Your number is actually higher and you have a typo.
   This is the single most important thing to fix; a reviewer will catch it in the first pass and reject on credibility grounds.

2. **"Strongest published result… to date" with a single prior baseline is overclaiming.** Even if 87.4 > Liu and Zhang, one comparator is not a literature sweep. Either soften ("outperforms the only prior published selective-classification benchmark on ChestX-ray14") or add more comparators (MC-dropout, standard softmax-threshold, deep ensemble reproduction, conformal prediction baselines).

3. **No baseline reproduction is reported.** You compare against a number from a 2019 paper but apparently did not re-run their method on your split/preprocessing. Differences in train/val split, image resolution, backbone, and label-cleanup (e.g., the relabeled NIH test set) can swing AUROC by several points. At minimum, reproduce a deep-ensemble + temperature-scaling baseline under your exact pipeline.

# Substantive issues

4. **"AUROC at 50% coverage" needs a precise definition.** AUROC is computed on a subset (the 50% retained by the selector). State explicitly: is this macro-averaged across 14 pathologies, computed per-pathology then averaged, or computed on a multi-label flattening? How is coverage defined for a multi-label problem — per-image, per-(image, pathology) pair, or per-pathology threshold? This is the central metric and is currently ambiguous.

5. **Selective-classification metrics beyond a single operating point.** Report the full risk–coverage curve and AURC (area under risk–coverage). A single point at 50% can be cherry-picked. Also report E-AURC or selective AUROC vs. coverage as a curve in the appendix.

6. **"At full coverage, the method matches the underlying classifier's accuracy."** You're doing multi-label AUROC, not accuracy — the wording slips into single-label framing. Say "matches the underlying classifier's per-pathology AUROC."

7. **"Largest improvements on Pneumonia and Edema, where label noise is highest" is asserted without support.** Either cite the label-noise characterization (e.g., Oakden-Rayner 2020 on NIH label quality) or drop the causal framing and say "the classes with the lowest baseline AUROC."

8. **Patient-level split — be explicit.** ChestX-ray14 has a known patient-overlap pitfall. State that you use the official patient-disjoint split (and confirm it; the original Wang et al. release had splits that some downstream work re-derived). One sentence suffices.

9. **"Eliminates the K-fold inference cost of ensembles while preserving uncertainty quality" — preserving relative to what?** You need a same-pipeline ensemble baseline to support "preserving." Otherwise weaken to "achieves competitive uncertainty estimates."

10. **Dirichlet-prior / evidential framing is under-described for related work.** If this is the related-work section, you should cite the evidential deep-learning lineage (Sensoy et al. 2018; Malinin and Gales 2018) rather than introducing it as if novel. Reviewers will ask.

# Smaller issues

11. "Pathology priors are skewed, calibration error is higher, and the cost of over-confident predictions is asymmetric" — three claims, zero citations. Add at least one (e.g., Rajpurkar et al. for class imbalance; Guo et al. 2017 for calibration of modern nets).
12. "Relatively sparse" is vague — name the gap. There are recent works on conformal prediction in radiology (e.g., Lu et al., Angelopoulos and Bates tutorial) that you should at least acknowledge to avoid the appearance of a thin lit review.
13. "RadAbstain" — first introduction of the name should be in bold or italics, and ideally the method section is forward-referenced.
14. Sentence "Their formulation has since been extended to natural-image domains" is filler; either cite an extension or cut it.
15. Wang et al. citation appears twice with no year on the second; standardize.

# Suggested rewrite of the results paragraph (illustrative)

> On the official patient-disjoint ChestX-ray14 test split, RadAbstain attains a macro-averaged per-pathology AUROC of 87.4% at 50% coverage and an AURC of [X], compared to [Y] for a same-backbone deep-ensemble + temperature-scaling baseline reproduced under our pipeline. At full coverage, per-pathology AUROC matches the non-selective classifier within ±0.2 points across all fourteen labels, indicating that the abstention head imposes no in-distribution penalty. The largest selective-coverage gains appear on Pneumonia and Edema, the two pathologies with the lowest non-selective baseline AUROC. We are not aware of a directly comparable published number on this benchmark; the closest prior result, Liu and Zhang (2019), reports [exact metric, exact split] and is not directly comparable because [reason].

# Uncertainty

I have not verified the Liu and Zhang (2019) reference — I could not find a paper matching that exact author/year/result in my training, and the contradiction with your headline number suggests the citation itself may be the issue. Please confirm it exists and that the 91.2% figure is accurate before keeping the comparison as written. I also have not verified the precise definition the field uses for "AUROC at X% coverage" on multi-label ChestX-ray14 — there is no single canonical convention, which is why item 4 matters.
